content.assessment: Migrated lens reading for 'Demographically-driven passive contraction (status-quo baseline)'. Because early selection is codified as a narrow, separately-justified legal exception rather than the structural default of secondary education, reform of the selection age is legally more tractable than reshaping Hungary's broader maintainer or curriculum architecture — but legitimacy and constitutional doctrine require any change to proceed on a felmenő-rendszer (grade-by-grade phase-in) basis with minimum ~1-year statutory lead time, protecting students already admitted under existing rules.
opportunities: Provides a clean counterfactual baseline against which the incremental effects of S1-S4 can be measured, isolating policy-driven change from demographically-driven change.

Reduces the absolute number of selective-track places over time simply through cohort shrinkage, without requiring any legislative or administrative action, avoiding all the implementation and reversal risks documented for S1-S4.
strengths: Hungarian law explicitly allows gymnasium (4, 6 or 8-grade forms) but treats the 6/8-year track as a distinct legal category requiring 'talent-nurturing' (tehetséggondozás) justification with separately regulated elevated standards, making selection age a codified exception rather than the structural default.

The Nkt. § 4 defines secondary education (középfokú nevelés-oktatás szakasza) as beginning at grade 9, with the 6- and 8-year gymnasium explicitly listed as the only statutory exception to this age boundary.
threats: Politically the path of least resistance, since it requires no legislative action and therefore faces no direct Fidesz culture-war framing risk, but this apparent political safety is itself a risk: it allows policymakers to avoid the equity problem indefinitely while claiming demographic forces are 'naturally' solving selectivity, when the evidence shows government is simultaneously using score thresholds (S2) as an active supply lever layered atop passive contraction.

Absence of active intervention risks EU-level criticism given the European Commission's explicit 2025 finding that Hungary's system amplifies social and economic inequalities, potentially triggering funds-conditionality pressure regardless of demographic trends.
weaknesses: Whether Hungary's Constitutional Court (Alkotmánybíróság) would treat abolition or significant raising of the 6/8-year selection age as a matter binding the felmenő-rendszer safeguard as mandatory constitutional doctrine, or whether it would classify it as a discretionary legislative choice subject only to rule-of-law process review.

Whether non-state maintainers (churches, foundations under remaining autonomy frameworks) would face differential legal constraints or political veto power in a national selection-age reform, and whether consensus-building with maintainer federations would constitute a hard prerequisite or a discretionary negotiation.
